topic: A person's invention is already patented by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the official document, the glossy paper feeling suddenly brittle beneath his fingertips. His breath hitched, a shallow, sharp intake of air that did little to fill his lungs. The title, his revolutionary 'Aetheric Resonator,' was listed under a patent number he recognized instantly—a number belonging to OmniCorp. He reread the fine print, his eyes darting back and forth across the dense legal jargon. A slow, creeping cold began to spread from his chest outward, numbing his hands. He pushed the papers away, the sound of the desk drawer closing echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet workshop. "Impossible," he whispered, the word tasting like ash. He walked to the window, the city lights below seeming distant and mocking, his knuckles white where he gripped the sill.

[story 2]

When Dr. Lena Hanson saw the stamped seal, a strange stillness settled over her. She leaned back in her chair, the leather groaning in protest. Her jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath her skin. For weeks, every late night, every sacrificed weekend, had been poured into refining the kinetic dampener. Now, it was simply filed away, a finished product already claimed. Lena picked up the blueprint, turning it over and over, her gaze tracing the familiar schematics with a detached, almost clinical focus. A tremor ran through her fingers, causing the paper to flutter slightly. She let out a slow, controlled exhale, the air leaving her lungs in a rush. "They anticipated it," she murmured to the empty lab, the sound barely audible over the hum of the ventilation system.

[story 3]

Marcus slammed his fist softly onto the workbench, the vibration rattling a jar of specialized resistors. He blinked rapidly, trying to force the information into a coherent shape. The patent notice lay flat on the wood, a stark white rectangle against the dark grain. His brow furrowed deeply, his gaze fixed on the section detailing the core mechanism—the very principle he had spent three years unlocking. He leaned in close, his nose nearly touching the paper, inhaling the faint, dry scent of aged ink. A sudden, sharp intake of breath caught in his throat. He straightened up abruptly, backing away until his spine hit the cold metal shelving. He shook his head once, a jerky, involuntary movement, as if trying to dislodge a faulty perception.
